title: The Shifting Drivers of Global Liquidity

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: This Staff Report examines how the sensitivity of international loan and bond flows to global factors—including U.S. monetary policy and global risk conditions—varied significantly over the post-financial crisis period. The research finds that sensitivity to U.S. monetary policy rose sharply after the crisis, peaked during the 2013 taper tantrum, then normalized, while the responsiveness of international bank lending to risk conditions declined steadily due to shifts in banking system composition.
key_points: Sensitivity of global liquidity components to U.S. monetary policy rose substantially in the immediate aftermath of the global financial crisis
Sensitivity peaked around the 2013 Fed 'taper tantrum' period, then reverted toward pre-crisis levels
Responsiveness of international bank lending to global risk conditions declined steadily throughout the post-crisis period
The degree of convergence among advanced economy monetary policies was the main driver of fluctuations in sensitivity to U.S. monetary policy
The post-crisis decline in sensitivity of international bank lending to global risk was driven by increases in lending shares of better-capitalized banking systems
Authors: Stefan Avdjiev, Leonardo Gambacorta, Linda S. Goldberg, and Stefano Schiaffi
why_it_matters: This research provides empirical evidence on how monetary policy transmission to global capital markets has evolved, with direct implications for understanding international spillovers of Fed policy. The findings suggest that global liquidity conditions become most sensitive to U.S. policy when monetary policy is divergent across economies, and that banking system health influences risk sensitivity in cross-border lending. Note: Source is historical (2017/2019); findings may predate more recent developments in global liquidity dynamics and post-pandemic monetary policy normalization.
macro_context: This research addresses macro-financial linkages through the lens of global liquidity dynamics and cross-border capital flows. It connects U.S. monetary policy to international lending and bond market conditions. The study period (pre- through post-crisis) provides historical context for understanding how policy sensitivity evolved, though the findings may not fully capture more recent market structures.
market_structure_context: The paper directly addresses market structure by analyzing the mechanics of international financial flows—specifically how bond and loan markets respond to monetary policy and risk conditions. It examines the composition of banking systems and how their capitalization levels affect cross-border lending behavior. This analysis remains relevant for understanding structural factors in global capital markets, though the data reflects pre-pandemic conditions.
